Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Plaintiffs Service Of Summons And To Dismiss Action For Lack Of In Personam Jurisdiction, Or, In The Alternative, To Dismiss Or Stay On Grounds Of Forum Non Conveniens Mpa
Off Calendar Mar-26-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
James J. Ficenec, Esq., Justin L. Clark, Esq., of Newmeyer & Dillion LLP, 925-988-3200 appearing for Plaintiff;
Sean E. Ponist, Esq., Ponist Law Group, Telephone 415-798-2222. appearing for Defendant Meco Constructors, Inc.;

RULING - A R G U E D. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Meco Constructors, Inc.'s motion to quash is denied. Meco is subject to the California forum selection clause in paragraph 13 of the "TERMS AND CONDITIONS OF AGREEMENT." The Court overrules Meco's objections to the Whitehouse declaration.

"He who takes the benefit must bear the burden" (Civ. Code 3521.) "A voluntary acceptance of the benefit of a transaction is equivalent to a consent to all the obligations arising from it, so far as the facts are known, or ought to be known, to the person accepting." (Id. 1589.) "It is clear that a party will not be permitted to receive and enjoy benefits that he knows are being offered to him at a price without paying for them, if he had an opportunity to reject them when they were offered without any expense or material inconvenience. Silence may here consummate his acceptance because the facts fairly call upon him to speak." (Durgin v. Kaplan (1968) 68 Cal.2d 81, 91.)

In this case, it is undisputed that plaintiff's consulting services were for Meco's benefit and Meco never objected to the provision of those services. Meco fails to meet its "heavy burden" of proving that enforcement of the forum selection clause would be unreasonable under the circumstances of the case. (See Bancomer, S.A. v. Superior Court (1996) 44 Cal.App.4th 1450, 1457.)

Meco's motion to stay or dismiss based on forum non conveniens is denied. Plaintiff's principal place of business is located in California. (Whitehouse Dec., 3.) The parties agreed to California jurisdiction and as a California resident, there is a strong presumption in favor of plaintiff's choice of forum. (See Ford Motor Co. v. Insurance Co. of North America (1995) 35 Cal.App.4th 604, 611; National Football League v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 902, 926-27.) Meco fails to show that the balance of public and private factors weighs in favor of granting its motion.

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: W. Trupek; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600 email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
